author: @kender1412
text:
Broadcast TV and radio is not publicly funded, except for PBS/NPR. The truth is, broadcasters PAY THE GOVERNMENT FOR their broadcast channels.

The idea of the FCC, in general, and the fair time policy, in particular, is that there are only a certain number of RF frequencies available to broadcast on.  It's further complicated because, even an audio broadcast, requires a range of frequencies.  Video takes more.  Then, you need a gap in between frequencies to keep channels from bleeding over into each other because both transmitters and receivers aren't perfect.

It isn't perfect, but it's necessary.  If there were no FCC regulations, there would be no broadcast TV.  There would be no broadcast radio.  Everybody who wanted to would be broadcasting all over each other and RF is NOT like the internet.  If I have my vlog, it doesn't interfere with your ability to have your vlog because I'm transmitting on the same frequency...

author: @jeffmaas9308
text:
"federally controlled" or "federally mandated" might be a better phrase than "federally funded" in cases like this.  While the US Federal government does provide money to public broadcaster (PBS stations) they do not to stations that host Colbert.  They provide the TV license for which they are paid,  they monitor and enforce regulations for which their costs are covered by the government but the airwaves and the network television stations are not using "federally funded" airwaves and thus "federally controlled" or "federally mandated" might be a better phrase.

Not being a dick not nit picking but I think it reduces your message when you overstate your message.
